ensemble.other_hypothesis: Mesenteric adenitis, ovarian pathology (torsion, cyst, tubo-ovarian abscess), infectious/inflammatory enterocolitis, Meckel's diverticulitis, intussusception
ensemble.information_gap: The anatomic source of the RLQ tenderness and bilateral lower quadrant pain has not been identified; no abdominal imaging has been performed, and the combination of RLQ tenderness, leukocytosis with neutrophilia (85.2%), heme-positive stool, and blood-streaked vomit requires structural/anatomic characterization.
ensemble.expected_finding: Periappendiceal fat stranding or appendiceal dilation consistent with appendicitis, or alternatively an adnexal mass/free fluid suggesting ovarian pathology, or bowel wall thickening suggesting enterocolitis or mesenteric adenitis
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has unexplained RLQ and bilateral lower quadrant pain with neutrophilia, heme-positive stool, blood-streaked emesis, and no prior abdominal imaging, making CT abdomen with contrast the appropriate next step to identify or exclude structural intra-abdominal pathology.
grounding: Physical examination: 'Soft, tender RLQ w/o rebound'
History: 'epigastric pain and bilateral lower quadrant pain'
Prior imaging 1: 'There is no acute intracranial hemorrhage, edema, mass effect' (head CT negative)
ensemble.reasoning: With a negative head CT, negative LP, negative UA, and negative pregnancy test already in hand, the remaining unexplained findings — RLQ tenderness, neutrophilia, heme-positive stool, and blood-streaked vomit — all point to an intra-abdominal process that has not yet been imaged. CT abdomen with contrast is the logical next step to characterize the appendix, bowel, mesentery, and pelvic organs simultaneously, given that this young woman has no prior abdominal imaging and the clinical picture does not clearly favor one diagnosis over another.

vindication.actual_finding: The appendix is mildly dilated to 9 mm with minimal wall thickening and air/fluid within the lumen, without periappendiceal fat stranding; adnexa and pelvis are otherwise unremarkable with only trace physiologic free fluid.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The report partially matches the expected finding by demonstrating appendiceal dilation (9 mm) with wall thickening, consistent with early or equivocal appendicitis, though the absence of periappendiceal stranding makes it less definitive; ovarian pathology and significant bowel wall thickening were not identified.
